Football.title: Neil Warnock replaced as Torquay boss with immediate effect after just 18 days
Football.content:
Neil Warnock replaced as Torquay boss with immediate effect after just 18 days

Neil Warnock has been replaced as manager of non-league side Torquay United.

The National League South side have confirmed the appointment of a new permanent manager.

3 Warnock has been replaced as Torquay manager after just 18 days in charge Credit: Shutterstock Editorial

It comes just 18 days after Warnock was placed in caretaker charge by the Gulls.



The 77-year-old had already been at the club as an advisor, a role he has held since May 2024.

However, he was put in charge of the first-team on March 1 following Paul Wotton's sacking.

Less than three weeks later, though, he has been replaced by new boss Jimmy Ball.

Torquay statement

In a statement confirming the appointment, Torquay said: "Torquay United AFC is delighted to announce the appointment of Jimmy Ball as the Club’s new First Team Manager.



"Jimmy joins United following a rigorous and considered recruitment process which attracted an incredible response from across the game.

"The Club received a high number of applications from very high-quality candidates, underlining both the size of the opportunity at Plainmoor and the enduring appeal of Torquay United.



"After an extensive process, the Board was unanimous in its belief that Jimmy was the outstanding fit for the role."

Ball's first match in charge will be away to Dagenham & Redbridge on Saturday.



Their hosts will also have a new man in the dugout after Andy Carroll was placed in interim charge on Wednesday.

3 The Gulls have confirmed Ball as their new permanent manager Credit: Shutterstock Editorial

Torquay currently sit fifth in the National League South, ten points adrift of leaders Dorking Wanderers.

Ball arrives from fellow sixth-tier side Totton, where he had been at the helm for the last four years.

He will now be hoping to secure a return to the National League for Torquay for the first time since relegation in 2023.



Warnock's Torquay record

Their hopes have been helped by Warnock's short-lived tenure as manager.

The former Sheffield United boss picked up seven points from his four league matches in charge.

Those included back-to-back home wins against Salisbury and Chesham United earlier this month.

3 Warnock picked up seven points from his four matches as caretaker manager Credit: Shutterstock Editorial

Meanwhile, his first match against Farnborough ended in a 2-2 draw.

The only defeat of his stint in the dugout came away to Ebbsfleet United on March 7.

News.title: ‘No victory without boots on the ground’, Trump warned after he says he’s ‘not afraid’ of Iran war becoming like Vietnam
News.content:
PRESIDENT Donald Trump has been warned there can be no win in Iran without a ground offensive, after the president declared he is not afraid of another Vietnam.

Kurdish commander Babasheikh Hosseini said the bloodthirsty Iranian regime will not fall without boots on the ground.





Hosseini gave the US a chilling ultimatum, saying: “If we are not on this battlefield, the end of the regime will either not occur, or be delayed by a lot.”

He told The Telegraph he wants US backing, saying: “We can still launch an invasion alone, but with their help it will be much better.”

It came as Trump told reporters that he did not fear another Vietnam.

He was asked in the Oval Office: “Are you afraid that if you put boots on the ground in Iran, it could be another Vietnam?”

The president shot back: “No. I’m not afraid of anything.”

More than 58,000 US soldiers were killed in the brutal Vietnam War, in a drawn-out conflict that lasted two decades.

Trump threatened to take out Tehran’s entire energy grid, after a dramatic exchange of blows at Middle Eastern gas plants yesterday sent oil prices rocketing.



He said: “We could take out their electric capacity in one hour.

“There’s nothing they can do.”

It comes as…

Hosseini heads up the Khabat Organisation, an exiled Iranian Kurdish armed group based in Iraq.

He said: “If our peshmerga were on the ground, and we told the people of Rojhelat to rise, then the fall of the regime would have been much closer by now.



The commander wants answers from Trump, who he said has given the Kurds mixed messages.

Asked about a Kurdish ground attack at the start of the war, the US President said: “I think it’s wonderful that they want to do that, I’d be all for ​it.”



Days later, he seemed to switch tack, saying: “The war is complicated enough without getting the Kurds involved.”

It comes as mysterious drones spotted over the military base in Washington that houses Trump’s top brass triggered security to be ramped up.

The unidentified unmanned aircraft were seen over a base home to Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, sparking serious security concerns.

Barrages of attacks continue in the Middle East as Iran trades blows with the US and its allies.

Yesterday, Iran threatened retaliation after Israeli strikes hit the South Pars gas field, which produces around three quarters of the regime’s oil.



The rogue nation hit back, hammering Qatar’s critical Ras Laffan LNG export hub with blows that caused “extensive damage”.

And the strikes saw a furious Donald Trump give a chilling warning.

The president wrote: “NO MORE ATTACKS WILL BE MADE BY ISRAEL pertaining to this extremely important and valuable South Pars Field unless Iran unwisely decides to attack a very innocent, in this case, Qatar.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“In which instance the United States of America, with or without the help or consent of Israel, will massively blow up the entirety of the South Pars Gas Field at an amount of strength and power that Iran has never seen or witnessed before.

“I do not want to authorize this level of violence and destruction because of the long term implications that it will have on the future of Iran, but if Qatar’s LNG is again attacked, I will not hesitate to do so.”

News.title: Brit ‘mum on the run’ who bragged about raking in benefits while living in luxury abroad is jailed
News.content:
A BRIT “mum on the run” who bragged about raking in benefits while living in luxury abroad has been jailed.

Ellis Matthews, 35, shot to online fame after fleeing the UK while pregnant and documenting her “dream life” in Thailand.





She also shared tips on how to fraudulently claim benefits – revealing she was raking in £2,300 a month while living overseas.

But Matthews was locked up in a “hell-hole” prison crawling with rats and cockroaches after overstaying her visa.

She was eventually deported back to Britain without her four-year-old son last year.

Within weeks of returning to Lancashire, Matthews was arrested over drug offences.



She has now been jailed for 26 months after pleading guilty to supplying cocaine, as well as possessing cocaine and cannabis.

Matthews was remanded to HMP Styal to begin her sentence but the 108 days she served in a Thai jail will count towards her jail term.

The wannabe influencer gained online notoriety with her bikini-clad “mum on the run” videos.

Matthews bragged about her “dream life” allegedly being funded by £2,300 per month in swindled British benefits.



She claimed to be living off Disability Living Allowance during her four years living abroad after telling the DWP she had six mental disorders.

Matthews now claims her TikTok videos about scamming benefits were “a joke” to “get more followers and make some money“.

She added: “The truth is I never got any UK benefits while in Thailand, not a single penny.

“It was all a big hoax, that’s all.

“I can understand why people were angry and upset about them.”

News.title: Teenage boy dies in devastating flat fire & another is rushed to hospital after escaping blaze
News.content:
FLAT BLAZE Teenage boy dies in devastating flat fire & another is rushed to hospital after escaping blaze

A TEENAGE boy has died after a horror blaze ripped through a flat with a second teen rushed to hospital.

The fatal fire broke out at a first floor flat of a six-storey building in Cooper’s Road in Southwark, London, just before 3pm yesterday.



One teenager was sadly pronounced dead at the scene, said the London Fire Brigade.

A second teenage boy left before firefighters arrived and was taken to hospital by London Ambulance Service crews.



Four fire engines and around 25 firefighters responded to the blaze on Cooper’s Road after being alerted.

Fire crews from Dockhead, Dowgate, Shadwell and Whitechapel fire stations attended the scene.

The blaze was brought under control just after 4.30pm on Wednesday.

The cause of the fire is being investigated by the Brigade’s Fire Investigation Unit and the Met Police.

News.title: We live on one of London’s most ‘Instagrammable’ streets – influencers & tourists turn our lives into a nightmare
News.content:
BLOOM & GLOOM We live on one of London’s most ‘Instagrammable’ streets – influencers & tourists turn our lives into a nightmare

RESIDENTS in a London Instagram hot spot say their lives are being ruined by swathes of tourists descending on their street.

Tourists and influencers amass upon the Japanese cherry trees when they come into bloom in Notting Hill, west London, every year.





But one stunning tree in particular has become the go-to backdrop for posing visitors and social media users this year.

Dozens of selfie stick influencers are visiting Stanley Crescent in droves every day – making life a nightmare for those who live there.

Residents on the street – where the average house price is £1.2 million – spoke of their horror as the tree came into bloom, bringing with it hundreds of tourists every week.

One local told The Sun: “It’s just absolutely ridiculous, we’ve been living here for 12 years and it’s just absolutely insane.”

The tourists can be seen bumbling around the middle of the road and posing on the wealthy residents’ doorsteps.

Supercars and delivery drivers are said to be seen having to weave between the many visitors in the street.

Another was seen taking selfies with the tree before dropping their lip gloss under an Aston Martin and using a selfie stick to retrieve it.



The neighbourhood has TikTok and Instagram to blame for the invasion of millennial snappers after the trend started in 2023.

Since then, hundreds of influencers post content from the tree every year in March and April.



But this year, the streets are more packed than ever with some visitors even hiring photographers for their trip.

The Sun spoke to family photographer Mira Malovana who was waiting to snap a mother and daughter who commissioned her to take photos under the tree.

She said: “Every spring I’m here, it’s more Japanese and Ukrainian people.”

“The locals are not happy about it, I saw one resident banging on the windows to stop a tourist climbing up their walls.”

Notting Hill is no stranger to influencers and tourists.



Some neighbours on the famously multi-coloured Lancaster Road began painting their houses white to avoid catching the attention of influencers.

Now it seems even influencers are getting fed up with the chaos.

Student Jerome Nguyen, 24, said: “I saw it on TikTok and Instagram so came here, I was going to shoot the trees but there’s just so many people”

“Obviously if I was a local I’d be very annoyed, I don’t think it’s disrespectful they’re just annoying.”

TikToker and Instagrammer Julia Belza, 23, comes every year to check out the tree in blossom.



She told The Sun: “I wish I could get pictures but I don’t have a chance now there’s so many people.

“On social media everyone hates it. But it’s their choice to live here, if they really hated it they could have cut down the tree.



“If you’re so rich you can live here then there’s some things you need to live with.”

The Sun saw several locals struggling to get past with push chairs as the tribe of tourists blocked pavements.

“You’d usually be able to come on a week day and there wouldn’t be anyone here but now I don’t know what’s happening,” said angry local Shaun, 39.

“If I was living in the house I’d be very upset.”

The internet’s obsession with Notting Hill dates back to the pre TikTok and Instagram era of 1999 with the release of aptly named movie, “Notting Hill.”



Starring the likes of Hugh Grant and Julia Roberts, the romcom went down as classic hit and secured Notting Hill’s popularity with influencers forever.

It comes just months after millionaires in the area became so fed up with influencers posing outside their homes they began painting them black.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Some residents in the Hillgate Village area of Notting Hill compared living on the iconic street to being trapped inside “Disneyland or the zoo”.

Locals in nearby Portobello Road have also complained about being trapped in their homes after it was repeatedly used as a filming location for Hollywood films and TV shows.

News.title: Amy Winehouse’s love rival, 47, who married singer’s ex-husband suffered ‘unnatural’ death, inquest told
News.content:
A MUM who married Amy Winehouse’s ex-husband suffered an “unnatural” death, an inquest heard today.

Sarah Aspin began dating the singer’s ex-husband, Blake Fielder-Civil, in 2011 after their tumultuous two-year marriage collapsed.





The couple met in rehab and went on to have two children together but it is understood Blake separated from Sarah in 2018.

An inquest heard how Sarah was found unresponsive at home in Leeds, West Yorkshire, on February 7 by her partner.

Paramedics were called but she was tragically declared dead at the scene.

The mum had high levels of prescription drugs in her system, including methadone, which is used to manage heroin addiction.

Wakefield Coroner’s Court the drugs were in a “combination of concentrations likely to have contributed to her death”.

Area Coroner Oliver Longstaff said Sarah’s death was not “natural” due to the large amount of medication in her system.

He said no formal cause of death has been given and adjourned the inquest for further enquiries.

The Sun told how police were scrambled to Sarah’s home in Leeds, West Yorkshire, last month following a concern for safety report.



Officers confirmed her death was being treated as “unexplained”.

A man found at the property was taken into custody for an offence not related to her death.

Paying tribute, Sarah’s daughter, Melody-Rose Hampshire, wrote online: “No words at all. Love you so mum.

“May you rest in eternal peace my beautiful mum.

“Doesn’t seem real. Just want you here and speak to you about your crazy antics.



“Life going to be so boring without you in it.”

Blake began a whirlwind romance with Amy when they met in 2005.

They married in Miami in 2007 but their relationship was plagued by problems over alcohol and drugs.

Sarah became embroiled in a love triangle with Amy – with the mum even warning the Rehab singer to “keep her hands off” Blake.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Just days later, tragic Amy was found dead from alcohol poisoning while Blake was starting a prison sentence for burglary and a firearm offence.



Sarah visited him in a private room following Amy’s death and told how he suffered continuous sobbing fits over her death and his inability to save her.

News.title: Fury as ‘ludicrous’ new town with 3,500 homes to be built next to beauty spot that inspired Tolkien’s Lord of the Rings
News.content:
TOWN & OUT Fury as ‘ludicrous’ new town with 3,500 homes to be built next to beauty spot that inspired Tolkien’s Lord of the Rings

LOCALS are fuming about plans to build a new settlement that could house 9,000 people in an area of natural beauty.

The proposed development, set to be called Glynchbrook, would sit within Gloucestershire, bordering Herefordshire and Worcestershire on the edge of the Forest of Dean.





The Forest of Dean District Council‘s plans include building 3,500 homes with no current plans to improve surrounding infrastructure, angering local residents.

Disturbing the sleepy landscape, the plans form part of the Government’s plans to build 1.5 million homes by 2029.

The Green-majority Forest of Dean council was set a target of building 13,000 new homes by 2045 by the government.



Both Herefordshire and Malvern District Councils have both voiced their opposition to the plans.

The surrounding Malvern Hills are believed to have inspired J.R.R Tolkien’s Lord of The Rings, making it understandable why residents are up in arms about disruption to the beautiful landscape.

A resident of nearby Bromsberrow, Andrew Elliot told the BBC: “It’s a travesty, it’s a horror story”.

“Quite simplistically, Glynchbrook should be taken out of the draft Local Plan, end of” he added.



Another major issue cited by locals is that the area is extremely prone to flooding, spending swathes of the year almost underwater.

Neil Goodall, 59, resident of neighbouring village Redmarley D’Abitot, told Mail Online that the plans were “ludicrous” due to the proposed developments location.

“It is absolutely in the wrong place.

“I understand that houses need to be built but they shouldn’t just be thrown somewhere because it’s an easy option,” he said.

Another resident, retired obstetrician and gynaecologist John Heap, 74, said: “It is ludicrous for a number of important planning and logical reasons,”



“Where is a whole pancake of concrete for the building of 3,500 houses going to shed water?

“It is going to flood even worse.

“And more than that, the sewage out of these houses is just going to end up floating in our lanes,” he explained.

The plans have followed a study led by the Forest of Dean council itself which showed that the site was ‘unlikely’ to meet the needed criteria, which is said to include ‘community, education, recreational, health and retail facilities’.

The report stated the land, which sits 16.5km from Gloucester and near the Cotswolds, would ’cause landscape harm; ‘potential harm to heritage’; ‘could lead to high reliability on transport by car’; and is ‘poorly’ located.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

A spokesperson for Forest of Dean District Council said: “No decisions have been made on any individual site at this stage.”

They added: “All potential locations are being assessed against a range of evidence, including flood risk, transport and access to services, landscape and environmental impacts, and sustainability considerations.”

News.title: Disturbing clue in missing James Gracey security video ‘points to foul play’ after Spring Breaker vanished outside club
News.content:
A WORRYING clue has been revealed in the disappearance of US university student James “Jimmy” Gracey who vanished outside a nightclub.

The revelation comes as police in Barcelona have today launched a search for the 20-year-old junior at sea, after activating their Aquatic and Underwater Units.





James was seen on CCTV footage leaving a nightclub with an unidentified person at about 3am on Tuesday, a reporter for the Spanish newspaper El Periódico told CBS 42.

Gisela Macedo also said that the Mossos d’Esquadra police force suspect that foul play might have been involved in his disappearance, because there has been no sign of the student since he left with the unknown individual.

The surveillance clip of James’s last moments at the club has not been released to the public, she added.

However, no persons of interest have been identified, Macedo also told the broadcaster.

READ MORE US NEWS SHARK HORROR Surfer mauled by shark after witnesses saw 'gigantic' beast lurking in water

Searches are being conducted around the vicinity of the club.

In a further update this morning, sources from the Mossos d’Esquadra (Catalan police) told El Periódico that the search has now widened to include the sea.

The city’s Aquatic and Underwater Units want to check whether he could have entered the water on Tuesday.



They are “trying to confirm or rule out the hypothesis given that the area of ​​nightclubs where he went out partying is located in front of the beach,” said El Periódico.

The publication confirmed that cops still don’t know who he left the premises with.

A fraternity brother of the University of Alabama student told ABC- affiliated WBMA that James had gone missing after becoming separated while at a nightclub in the city’s vibrant Ciutat Vella district.

The Illinois man disappeared while visiting friends during a Spring break trip with pals.



“Unfortunately, he was separated from the rest of the group later into the night, and that was the last time we’ve heard from him,” Cavin McLay sad yesterday.

James was reported missing by his distraught family after he failed to turn up at the AirBnb he had booked for March 16.

His mom, Therese Gracey, said in an urgent Facebook post that he was last seen by friends at Shoko nightclub at about 3am on Tuesday.

Police have already searched the accommodation, according to El Periódico.



Beth O’Reilly, an aunt, told News Nation the family remained hopeful that James would be found safe.

“We’re doing the best we can, taking it minute by minute,” she said.

“We’re all just hanging together and doing the best we can to get his story out so people see his face, recognize his face, and if they know anything, that they’ll bring it to the authorities.”

O’Reilly said the family was worried that James would not be able to contact anyone as he doesn’t have his phone.

It’s believed that police retrieved it from the nightclub.



“The reason we’re so concerned [is that] he does not have his phone, and he is not known to not be in touch with family and friends,” she added.

The Gracey family's latest statement on James 'Jimmy' Gracey missing in Barcelona “Jimmy is a 20-year-old University of Alabama student visiting friends abroad during spring break. “He was last seen around 3:00 AM CET at the Shoko nightclub in the Port Olímpic area. “He was wearing a white T-shirt, dark pants (likely joggers), and a gold chain with a rhinestone cross. He is approximately 6’1” and 175 pounds. “We are asking anyone who may have seen Jimmy or has any information — no matter how small — to please contact 224.505.3886. “Jimmy is a kind, responsible, and devoted son and brother. It is completely out of character for him not to check in with family and friends. “We are working closely with local authorities and are incredibly grateful for the support we have received. “We ask that you keep Jimmy in your thoughts as we continue to do everything we can to bring him home safely.”

The Mossos d’Esquadra police has not yet confirmed that cops have his cellphone, said El Periódico.

James’s family has now released two statements asking for help to find him.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

His mom said on Tuesday that he was believed to have been wearing a white T-shirt and dark pants — likely joggers — at the time of his disappearance.

Both of his parents have flown to Spain to join the search.

News.title: Vile domestic abusers to be TRACKED with tags that tell cops if they’re near victims under new £100m plans
News.content:
DOMESTIC abusers will be tracked in real time under new plans with tags able to alert police if they get too close to their victims.

The new tech is part of new Government plans to ensure almost all lags will be tagged when they leave prison in a huge overhaul of the probation system.



Prisons minister Lord James Timpson has announced “the biggest expansion of tagging in British history” with more funding for overstretched probation workers.



But the plan will also see fewer appointments for lower level offenders – as data shows almost a third of all meetings are already not going ahead due to overstretched resources.

The move comes after Labour announced that criminals will spend as little as a third of their sentences behind bars and instead serve the rest of their time in the community.

The £100 million expansion includes greater use of GPS and alcohol monitoring, as well as proximity tags to keep domestic abusers away from their victims.

The tag will alert police if abusers come within a certain distance of their victims and cops will contact the offender to tell them to move away.

Police could be dispatched to apprehend them if they refuse.

Lord Timpson said the expansion of tagging would mean “the most dangerous offenders will now be watched more closely than ever before”.

Last year The Sun saw how prisoners will be tagged within the prison walls during a trial of the process at Wormwood Scrubs.

As well as a presumption that offenders leaving prison will be tagged, the Ministry of Justice will invest £8 million in new technology aimed at cutting administrative tasks.



That includes the use of the MoJ’s in-house AI tool, Justice Transcribe, to take notes during probation officers’ meetings with the offenders they supervise.

Some 246,502 people were being supervised by the probation service in September last year.

Lord Timpson said: “By combining new technology with a stronger probation workforce, we’re making sure those who pose the biggest risk are under constant scrutiny to better protect victims and the public.”

Martin Jones, the chief inspector of probation, said he welcomed the proposals and the bid to “focus time and resources where they matter most”.

He said: “We are entering a crucial period as the implementation of the Sentencing Act reforms begins.



“There must be a sharp focus on ensuring the probation service can recruit, train, and retain sufficient staff, and give them the tools and support they need – both to keep the public and victims safe, and to turn offenders’ lives around.”

Victims commissioner Claire Waxman also welcomed the proposals, but said tagging should be “backed by swift, robust enforcement the moment a breach occurs” or risk creating a “false sense of security for victims”.

She added: “While prioritising high-risk offenders is a pragmatic necessity, it is essential that ‘lower-risk’ is never treated as ‘no-risk’. Ultimately, victim safety must remain the priority.”

Pia Sinha, chief executive of the Prison Reform Trust, warned the presumption that all offenders would be tagged on leaving prison could “undermine” efforts to reduce pressure on prisons.

She said “Electronic monitoring is not a panacea for reducing reoffending.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

“While it can be a useful option as part of licence conditions, evidence shows it is most effective when combined with appropriate supervision and support.

“Tagging can be an effective tool for enhancing monitoring and supervision, but it also carries the risk of increased stigmatisation which can undermine resettlement and reintegration on release.”

News.title: Netflix boss issues defiant update on damning claims about Harry and Meghan’s deal
News.content:
Netflix boss issues defiant update on damning claims about Harry and Meghan’s deal

The streaming giant’s chief content officer has hit back at recent claims that the partnership between Netflix and the Sussexes was deteriorating - but another insider tells a different story

View 5 Images Prince Harry and Meghan Markle signed a new deal with Netflix in 2025 (Image: John Angelillo/UPI/Shutterstock)

A Netflix executive has hit back at recent damning claims that the relationship between the streaming giant and the Sussexes is in jeopardy, after a scathing report was issued from a group of insiders.

‌



A series of sources told Variety yesterday that the Netflix partnership with Prince Harry and Meghan Markle was deteriorating, after the streaming giant has parted ways with Meghan’s brand As Ever, which had received much financial help from Netflix since its inception in early 2025. The insiders claim that their deal had suffered, as claims were made that the Sussexes were difficult to deal with.

‌



Meghan Markle gives glimpse of Princess Lilibet as she helps plug new £200 gift boxes READ MORE :

Kate Middleton dazzles at state banquet in Diana's favourite tiara READ MORE :

View 5 Images A Netflix executive has defended the Sussexes after a scathing report (Image: Getty Images)

‌



Now, Netflix chief content officer Bela Bajaria has publicly denied the claims of a dispute, briefly addressing the speculation at the Next on Netflix event on Wednesday.

Bajaria reiterated that Netflix still has a partnership with the couple, saying: "We still have a relationship with them. We have movies in development with them. They have things in development on the TV and film side.”

She added that Netflix “had an amazing documentary with them,” referring to their six-part docuseries titled Harry & Meghan, which was released in December 2022, and documented the early days of the relationship and their decision to step back from their royal roles.

‌



View 5 Images The Sussexes' six-part docuseries with the streamer aired in 2022 (Image: Netflix)

Bajaria added of their deal: "Deals come and go all the time, and we don’t renew so many deals, those just don’t get as much press for obvious reasons. There’s no juicy story there." Despite Bajaria’s claims, another Netflix insider told the Daily Mail’s Alison Boshoff that staff at the streamer would not air their grievances with the couple in public.

The source said: “No one is willing to be other than supportive in public” as they added that there is potential for a new season of With Love, Meghan, however: “I don’t think anyone is going to be killing themselves with an effort to work with them at this point.”

‌



Meghan and Harry signed a "multi-year, first look deal for film and television projects" with Netflix in 2025, which was considered a downgrade from their previous deal, signed in 2020, which was reported to be more than $100 million (£74 million).

Bajaria’s comments come just hours after Variety published a scathing analysis into the Sussexes relationship with Netflix, in which several inside sources exposed what went on behind the scenes when Harry and Meghan first signed on with the streamer, and how their partnership had deteriorated over time.

View 5 Images Insider sources told Variety that the couple were poor communicators

‌



A Netflix insider claimed that the Duke and Duchess of Sussex had “ruffled feathers” in meetings, while also claiming Archewell Productions has a long history of “poor communication” with outside companies.

According to Variety, insiders claim that in virtual and in-person meetings with Netflix, Meghan tends to talk over or recast Harry’s thoughts, sometimes while he is mid-sentence, usually preceded by a touch to the arm or thigh.

Meghan’s lawyer Michael J. Kump hit back at the claims, telling Variety that this assertion “seems calculated to play into the misogynistic characterization of her bossing her husband around.” Prince Harry, meanwhile, attests that this is “categorically false”.

‌



The report also claims that Netflix bosses were blindsided by the Sussex’s bombshell interview with Oprah in 2021, and the release of Harry’s memoir Spare in 2023.

View 5 Images Sources said Netflix bosses were blindsided by Harry and Meghan's interview with Oprah

‌



A Netflix insider says the plan was always to air the Harry & Meghan docuseries in December 2022, though two sources say plans ultimately changed to get ahead of the memoir, which was released less than a month later. A spokesman for the Sussexes insists they co-operated with Netflix throughout.

According to an inside Netflix source, the collaboration between Meghan and Harry and the streamer has been far from a fairy tale as they told Variety that the mood on the Sussexes has shifted with the streamer, saying: “The mood in the building is ‘We’re done.’”

The Variety article also suggested that the relationship between Prince Harry and Meghan Markle and Netflix has cooled, though a spokesperson for the streamer told the outlet it is “absolutely inaccurate” that CEO Ted Sarandos and Chief Content Officer Bela Bajaria have lost faith in the couple. Kump added that the couple remains friendly with Sarandos and his wife, Nicole Avant, and are in regular contact, including visits to their Montecito home.

Article continues below

News.title: Donald Trump's Iran war in chaos as spy chief who quit blames Israel for 'false intel'
News.content:
Donald Trump's Iran war in chaos as spy chief who quit blames Israel for 'false intel'

Donald Trump’s top counterterrorism spy claims Israel pushed the US into a war with Iran based on flawed intelligence, while dissent was shut down inside the White House

View 5 Images A senior US intelligence chief quits in protest, claiming Donald Trump was pushed into war with Iran by Israeli pressure and “false intelligence” while dissent inside the White House was shut down (Image: White House via Getty Images)

Donald Trump’s top counterterrorism spy, who quit over the Iran war, has blamed Israel for dragging the United States into the conflict.

‌



Joe Kent warned that targeting Iran’s leadership risked backfiring, arguing the removal of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei could usher in a more extreme regime. The former counterterrorism chief said Khamenei had been acting as a restraint on nuclear escalation, adding: “Going aggressively after the Ayatollah was the last thing we should have ever done.”

‌



“This speaks to the broader issue: who is in charge of our policy in the Middle East? Who is in charge of when we decide to go to war or not?” he said. Kent repeated his claim that Iran posed no direct threat to the United States and suggested the conflict was undertaken on Israel’s behalf, pointing to public comments made by senior figures, including Marco Rubio.

‌



View 5 Images Resigned counterterrorism chief Joe Kent says Iran posed no imminent threat and warns the US was drawn into conflict on behalf of others (Image: Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Kent issued a blistering resignation letter on Tuesday in which he said Iran posed no imminent threat and claimed the conflict was driven by pressure from Israel rather than American interests.

‌



In a series of explosive remarks, the former Director of the National Counterterrorism Centre has now said internal debate had effectively been shut down before the decision to strike. “Key decision makers were not allowed to express their opinions. There wasn't a robust debate,” Kent told Tucker Carlson.

He described a White House where the outcome appeared predetermined, adding: “It seemed to be a foregone conclusion that this was happening.” He argued that Trump should have pursued diplomacy rather than military escalation, suggesting the US could have established a backchannel with Tehran while allowing Israel to act independently if it chose.

“I think there's a potential there where we could have done several different things, we could have simply said to the Israelis, ‘No you will not, and if you do, we will take something away from you,’” he said.

‌



He also cast doubt on the reliability of intelligence provided by Tel Aviv, claiming: “Israeli officials… will say all kind of things that simply isn't true.” Kent said he had less access to Trump than the Israeli Prime Minister, noting “it did seem like Benjamin Netanyahu was in the White House quite a bit” compared with himself and Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard.

View 5 Images Donald Trump meets Benjamin Netanyahu at the White House, as claims swirl that Israel drove the decision to launch the war with Iran (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

Without naming individuals, he alleged that anyone who opposed Trump was effectively sidelined and unable to brief the president directly.

‌



He added that statements from Trump, Rubio and House Speaker Mike Johnson indicated “the Israelis drove the decision to take this action, which we knew would set off a series of events, meaning the Iranians would retaliate.” While insisting the US-Israel alliance should continue, Kent said Washington must retain control over how its military support is used.

“It’s fine that we offer defence to Israel, but when we’re providing the means of defence, we get to dictate the terms of when they go on the offensive; otherwise, they stand to lose that relationship,” he said. “The Israelis felt emboldened that no matter what they did, they could take this action, and we would just have to react.” He also alleged the presence of “a lobby” pushing for military action.

Kent, an Iraq War veteran, said his decision to resign was unavoidable as casualties mounted. “For me personally, watching more casualties come in, I just couldn't stand by and continue to soldier on in this, it's time to try something different. I've been on this path before, I've seen the data,” he said.

‌



Kent urged Trump to return to the non-interventionist platform he campaigned on in 2024, calling on him to “take a look and assess” the situation and recommit to a policy of avoiding new wars in the Middle East.

In his resignation letter, he accused the President of abandoning those principles. “Until June of 2025, you understood that the wars in the Middle East were a trap that robbed America of the precious lives of our patriots and depleted the wealth and prosperity of our nation,” he wrote.

“The time for bold action is now. You can reverse course and chart a new path for our nation, or you can allow us to slip further toward decline and chaos. You hold the cards.”

‌



View 5 Images Explosions light up Tehran as US strikes escalate a conflict that has already cost lives and sent shockwaves across the Middle East (Image: Getty)

Trump dismissed the criticism, telling reporters it was a “good thing that [Kent's] out,” and branding him “very weak on security.”

His departure exposes a deepening rift within Trump’s political movement. He is closely aligned with the “America First” faction, including Vice President JD Vance and intelligence chief Tulsi Gabbard, both of whom have warned against deeper involvement in Middle Eastern conflicts.

‌



His claims that Israeli officials and parts of the US media ran a “misinformation campaign” to exaggerate the threat from Iran echo comparisons to the flawed intelligence that preceded the Iraq War.

At least 13 US troops have been killed, with hundreds more injured across seven countries, while global energy markets have been thrown into turmoil. Gas prices have surged from $2.90 to an average of $3.80 a gallon in just three weeks. Kent’s resignation has drawn sharp reactions across the political spectrum.

View 5 Images Donald Trump watches as the bodies of fallen US troops are returned - as criticism mounts over a war insiders say should never have been fought (Image: White House/News Pictures/Shutterstock)

‌



Former Congresswoman Marjorie Taylor Greene described him as “a great American hero,” while right-wing commentator Candace Owens branded Trump “a shameful President” and urged troops to consider conscientious objection, calling Kent a “patriot.”

Others were less sympathetic. Pro-Israel activist Laura Loomer labelled him a “notorious leaker” and suggested his departure was timed to overshadow Gabbard’s scheduled testimony before Congressional intelligence committees.

She also reignited a long-running feud, calling Kent a “Tucker Carlson acolyte who undermines President Trump every chance he gets.” Kent, 45, is a decorated former Green Beret who served 11 combat tours before joining the CIA as a paramilitary officer. His wife, Navy Senior Chief Petty Officer Shannon Kent, was killed in a suicide bombing in Syria, leaving him to raise their two children.

Article continues below

He later entered politics as an outspoken critic of US military intervention, running for Congress.

News.title: Covid Inquiry report LIVE as warnings UK not ready for new pandemic
News.content:
The harrowing details of the extent the coronavirus pandemic had on our NHS is set to be revealed today as a new report is released.

The Covid-19 Public Inquiry is set to give its verdict on how our country's healthcare system handled the global pandemic and the impact the virus had on both patients and staff. During the inquiry, health leaders broke down as they gave powerful testimony, including one senior medic who was in tears as he described scenes "from hell" on intensive care wards.

Professor Kevin Fong, former national clinical adviser in emergency preparedness, resilience and response at NHS England, described the rate of deaths on intensive care units as "truly astounding", saying that one hospital he visited appeared close to collapse. Over the course of two-and-a-half years, the biggest public inquiry in British history has heard harrowing testimony from various witnesses.

Previous modules found that Boris Johnson's government cost thousands of lives by acting too late. Baroness Hallett concluded that the Partygate Prime Minister oversaw a "toxic culture" as bereaved families slammed his cabinet's "catastrophic mishandling" of the pandemic.

Former health secretary Matt Hancock gave evidence at the inquiry, where he shared that England's hospitals were just "hours" away from running out of some personal protective equipment (PPE) during the early months of the pandemic. He also said he "reluctantly" approved the decision to pause non-urgent planned care during the pandemic.

Follow our live blog below for all the latest updates:

News.title: Dr Amir Khan says long Covid can affect your brain, lungs, joints and skin
News.content:
Dr Amir Khan says long Covid can affect your brain, lungs, joints and skin

The NHS GP and TV doctor discussed how long Covid can affect multiple systems during a podcast appearance

View 2 Images Dr Amir Khan answered a question about the health condition (Image: ITV)

Dr Amir Khan has explained why people with long Covid may experience different symptoms, breaking down how the condition can affect multiple symptoms. Long Covid, or post-Covid syndrome, occurs when Covid-19 symptoms last for longer than twelve weeks, according to the NHS.

‌



Signs of long Covid may include fatigue, joint pain, shortness of breath, or brain fog. While treatment is available, and anyone with health concerns should talk to a GP, Dr Amir Khan says more investment is needed to better understand and manage the condition.

‌



The NHS GP and TV doctor spoke about the difficulties involved with treating long Covid patients during an appearance on the Bold Politics podcast. During the conversation, Dr Amir was asked about long Covid patients who might feel they have been 'left behind' in public discourse, and the doctor said he empathises with patients dealing with the condition, which currently has no cure.

‌



Dr Amir said: "Long Covid is a really poorly understood condition, and it's a multi-system complex issue, and it will affect everyone differently. You know, everyone has a different version of long Covid because it is so multisystem, it is so inflammatory. It can cause so many different things to go wrong at the same time, and you're left with these conditions that nobody really wants to touch because there hasn't been enough investment in the understanding, but also the management."

He claimed: "There used to be a long Covid service at most hospitals where it was run by the respiratory team, perhaps, and they would take care of it. All of that seems to have just melted away. And because, like I said, long Covid can affect your brain, your lungs, your skin, your joints, it can affect so many things, your mental health, but also how fatigued you are, we haven't got one group of people looking after it."

Content cannot be displayed without consent

‌



The GP explained that if he saw a patient presenting with long Covid, he would need to refer them to multiple departments. For instance, someone might need to see a rheumatologist for joint pain and a respiratory specialist for their lungs."

Dr Amir said it is a 'real problem' and that he feels 'really helpless' when helping patients with the condition. "We've got these people who were really well and healthy a few years ago who are now, you know, very affected by their symptoms, and a lot of them have lost their independence and their ability to earn as a result of it. We haven't got anything in place to look after them."

He added: "We're talking about adults, but it can affect children as well. And so, it's a real problem. They come in, I feel really helpless when I see them, to be honest, because I know it's one of those consultations where I know, yes, I can listen to them and yes, I can empathise with them.

Article continues below

View 2 Images Long Covid can cause shortness of breath and fatigue (stock photo) (Image: Getty)

"But there's very limited things I can do to help them because giving them painkillers isn't going to be the long-term answer. And we don't know enough about the condition to know which specific medication might help reverse some of their symptoms. And that's a massive issue for them. So they have been left in limbo."

More information about symptoms is available on the NHS website. Information for anyone affected by long Covid is available on www.longcovidsos.org and Long COVID SOS: resources.

News.title: 'I worked for Royal Family – Andrew trait made me dread being around him'
News.content:
'I worked for Royal Family – Andrew trait made me dread being around him'

As a former royal butler, it stands to reason that Grant Harrold encountered the King’s disgraced brother on occasion, and judging by his account, Andrew exuded a certain "vibe"

View 3 Images Grant Harrold recalled how Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor had a "vibe" (Image: Chris Jackson/Getty Images)

A former butler who served the Royal Family has recalled how Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor had a "vibe" that made him not want to "be in his presence". Having worked for the likes of King Charles and Queen Camilla and his sons, Harry and William, it stands to reason that Grant Harrold was privy to some of the innermost workings of the Royal household.

Given his position, it's also safe to assume that he shared an interaction or two with the King's brother. The disgraced former prince has dominated headlines due to a highly publicised fall from grace stemming from his links to the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

Thames Valley Police arrested Andrew on suspicion of misconduct in public office last month on his 66th birthday. Andrew, who continues to deny any wrongdoing, was released under investigation.

When asked to share his thoughts on the disgraced former prince, Grant explained that he had limited interactions with Andrew, but conceded that when he did, he was "polite", yet there was a discernible "entitled feeling."

View 3 Images Grant said there was a discernible "entitled feeling” (Image: PR HANDOUT)

He told LADbible Stories: "You know, there would be a kind of thank you or whatever, but there was also this feeling of, as a lot of people describe – entitled. You got the feeling he thought he was better than everyone, just his mannerisms and his...the way he was.

"And what was weird for me was no other member of the family did you get that vibe from, not from the Queen, not from Prince Philip, not from his brother, Charles, not from Anne, none of them.

"But with him, he had this, I can't even explain it to you. I just didn't want to be in his presence. I mean, you know, luckily, I didn't have to do much for him, and as I said, I didn't have..."

View 3 Images He alleged that none of his royal household colleagues "ever have a nice thing to say" about Andrew (Image: PR HANDOUT)

Grant then recounted the King and Queen's wedding, claiming that Andrew "didn't speak" to staff members attending as guests, and would only converse with family or family friends.

He described this as "sad", conceding that he didn't know "why he was like that". None of his royal household colleagues "ever have a nice thing to say" about him, Grant further alleged.

Andrew wasn't "one of the most popular royals", he added, noting that, in his opinion, he doesn't recall them "being that close". Yet, Grant pointed out that Charles is "absolutely" close to his sister, Anne.

Article continues below

He theorised that perhaps the King always had an "inkling", adding that he got the impression that they weren't "particularly close".

News.title: Putin's allies predict 'end of world nuclear war' and slam 'satanic' Trump
News.content:
Vladimir Putin’s propagandists have said the US operation in Iran is “absolute evil” and warned of an “end times” nuclear war.

Russian state TV host Vladimir Solovyov said “what we are observing is pure Satanism” on his show, where he discussed the fallout of the US-Israeli strikes on Iran, claiming the Islamic Republic could “completely destroy Israel in its entirety” if it wanted.

“With what is happening in America it is clear that nuclear weapons will be used,” Vladimir Solovyov told guests on his show. “Trump is already talking about it openly. I’ve been talking about it for several years. If we keep thinking ‘No, it isn’t possible,’ it won’t help us.

“The scariest thing that will happen is that Trump will use nuclear weapons and understand that this is not a cure-all. The size of Iran as a country is such that regardless of what kind of nuclear weapons you use… afterwards Iran would respond and the last restriction will fall.

Image: Russian Media Monitor) Russian Media Monitor)

“The world will move into a phase when everyone who has it will trade nuclear strikes. This is a totally different life. The level of escalation will increase momentarily. Are we including this in our calculations? I hope so.”

He went on: “It became absolutely clear that America cannot achieve its goals as quickly and simply as they thought… Trump declared every day that they’ve already won but every day Iran keeps striking back.”

He warned that Iran could “completely destroy Israel in its entirety with its final strike.”

“All of it can disappear forever, of course this would be a frightening omen. And this is very realistic. What we are observing is pure Satanism, it's reigning in the world”. He added that what America is doing right now is “absolute evil”.

His guest Margarita Simonyan, head of RT, agreed. She said: “We live in penultimate times. Now there will be a nuclear war and then it will be the end times... In the history of the world it was never so evident as it is right now

“When we get out of all this I think we'll be horrified about what we’ll end up being surrounded with. I think we’ll watch Sodom and Gomorrah burn with a blue flame... God willing this flame won’t touch us and we can watch this sight while snacking on pickles.”

News.title: UK passport holders face major fee change on April 8 2026 'for the first time'
News.content:
UK passport holders face major fee change on April 8 2026 'for the first time'

People will need to pay more for their passports from next month

View 3 Images Cost of UK passport to exceed £100 for first time (Image: Getty )

Brits renewing their passports are set to face new fee changes in the coming weeks, as the government is due to introduce updated application costs on April 8, 2026. The price of a standard UK passport will exceed £100 for the first time next month as fees are rising by 8%, the Home Office has announced.

‌



The cost of online applications made within the UK for people aged 16 and above will increase from £94.50 to £102. Passports for children under 16 will cost £66.50, up from £61.50 currently.

‌



There are similar percentage rises in fees for postal applications and those made from overseas. The changes, which are subject to parliamentary approval, are due to come into force on April 8.

‌



They follow increases of 7% in each of the last two years, and 9% in 2023. The proposals, which, again, are subject to approval by Parliament, will include the following:

The standard online application made from within the UK will rise from £94.50 to £102 for adults.

It will go up from £61.50 to £66.50 for children, too.

Postal applications will increase from £107 to £115.50 for adults and £74 to £80 for children.

The fee for a Premium Service (one-day) application made from within the UK will rise from £222 to £239.50.

The fee for a standard online application for a UK adult passport when applying from overseas will rise from £108 to £116.50.

It will also increase from £70 to £75.50 for children.

Overseas standard paper applications will increase from £120.50 to £130 for adults and £82.50 to £89 for children.

View 3 Images Changes to passport application fees will take effect on April8, 2026

‌



The Home Office said in a statement: "The new fees will help the Home Office to continue to move towards a system that meets its costs through those who use it, reducing reliance on funding from general taxation.

"The Government does not make any profit from the cost of passport applications. The fees contribute to the cost of processing passport applications, consular support overseas, including for lost or stolen passports, and the cost of processing British citizens at UK borders."

According to Gov.uk, the new fees will help the Home Office continue to move towards a system that covers its costs through those who use it, reducing reliance on general taxation. The government does not make a profit from passport application costs.

Article continues below

View 3 Images The proposals are subject to approval by Parliament

The fees contribute to the cost of processing passport applications, consular support overseas, including for lost or stolen passports, and processing British citizens at UK borders.

Customers are advised to apply well in advance of travelling. Apply online for a UK passport. In 2025, where no further information was required, 99.7% of standard applications from the UK were processed within three weeks.

News.title: Full list of areas where meningitis outbreak has spread MAPPED after more cases confirmed
News.content:
Full list of areas where meningitis outbreak has spread MAPPED after more cases confirmed

The map shows where meningitis cases have been recorded in the UK after the number of cases soared to 27, as health officials battle to contain the Kent outbreak

View 5 Images Meningitis jabs are being given to at-risk Kent university students (Image: PA)

A new map shows exactly where cases of meningitis have been confirmed amid fears the 'unprecedented' outbreak could spread across the country.

‌



Health officials this morning confirmed that the number of cases of meningitis linked to a nightclub in Kent has risen to 27 - including two young people who sadly died from the infection.

‌



The 27 cases either confirmed or under investigation represent a rise on the 20 reported on Wednesday, with nine of the 15 confirmed cases confirmed to have be caused by menB, a deadly bacterial form of the disease.

‌



Trish Mannes, UKHSA regional deputy director for the South East, said it is important students still come forward for antibiotics after thousands of doses have been handed out to those who attended Club Chemistry in Canterbury from March 5-7, alongside students living in halls at the Canterbury campus of the University of Kent.

Around 5,000 students have also been invited for a menB vaccine.

View 5 Images Sixth form pupil Juliette, 18, died surrounded by her family

‌



Meningitis vaccine - who can get it amid national shortages READ MORE :

Meningitis map

Check out our meningitis map below:

View 5 Images Cases have been confirmed in London and Dorset (Image: Datawrapper)

Areas with confirmed meningitis cases:

Faversham, Kent

Ashford, Kent

North Greenwich, London

Weymouth, Dorset

France (region not disclosed)

‌



Several schools in Kent have reported cases connected to their pupils, including Queen Elizabeth's Grammar School in Faversham, Norton Knatchbull School in Ashford, Simon Langton Grammar School for Boys in Canterbury, and Highworth Grammar School in Ashford.

The UKHSA is also investigating a case in North Greenwich after a student was hospitalised with the infection.

View 5 Images Infections can be spread through close personal contact (Image: GETTY)

‌



'We're not in the position to say it's been contained'

This morning, Kent County Council’s director of public health Dr Anjan Ghosh told BBC Radio 4’s Today programme it could not be said the outbreak has been contained.

“We’re not in the position yet to say that definitively, that it’s been contained,” he said.

“If you see the daily reporting that’s going on, there are more and more cases being reported, but these cases all relate more or less to that same period of time when the initial exposure happened.

‌



“We are looking at what’s called secondary transmission, so that’s a case that’s then transmitted to another couple of people. We need to rule that out before we can say it’s definitely contained.”

View 5 Images Anyone who attended Club Chemistry in Canterbury between March 5 and 7 is being urged to come forward for antibiotics (Image: PA)

But reassuring the public that we are not about to witness a repeat of the Covid pandemic, he added: “The main advice is there’s no reason to be anxious.

‌



“This is a disease. It’s not Covid. It doesn’t spread the same way that Covid or measles spread.

“It spreads through close, protracted contact, intimate contact. It’s basically people in households, sharing cups, kissing, intimate contact, those kind of things.

“There’s no need to panic or get anxious. People just need to go about their ordinary lives the way they have been.

Article continues below

“However, if people have signs of meningitis, then they need to act fast.”

News.title: How Europe sleepwalked into yet another energy crisis
News.content: Energy-hungry Germany gets as much as 96% of its LNG from the US now. This dependence might explain why the German Chancellor Friedrich Merz remained silent when he sat next to Trump in the White House two weeks ago, as the US president berated and threatened to impose a trade embargo on Spain, because it had not allowed him to use military bases on its territory to launch attacks on Iran.

News.title: World's longest coastal path opens in England
News.content: For him, the length of the path isn't really the point. What makes it so special, he says, is that you can walk to the coast anywhere in England, turn left or right, and walk beside the sea for as long as you like.

News.content: The study said that as the cocker spaniel, for instance, was designed to flush out game from dense areas of woodland and grassland, the dogs were bred to have high energy, mental resilience and intelligence, which could be "more of a problem in pet homes".

News.content: "They should've got me in straight away, they should've seen me as soon as I turned up to hospital because I was classed as high risk, they should have listened to the midwives that were watching that CTG (cardiotocography) quite closely but everything got overruled [by the doctors]," she told the BBC.

News.content: The two sovereign bases in the south and south-east of Cyprus have not been used for US operations against Iran, although the UK has since granted the US permission to use them for "specific defensive operations".

News.content: Prior to Trump's statement, the Wall Street Journal had quoted unnamed US officials saying the president had supported the strike on South Pars as a message to Iran over its restriction of tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz - one of the world's busiest oil shipping lanes - but that he did not want to see further such strikes.

Boxing.title: The moment Tyson Fury tells raging wife Paris he's coming out of boxing retirement - and says she should seek anger management - as she calls him a 't**t'
Boxing.content:
Tyson Fury might already have one eye on his looming return to the ring but the latest trailer for At Home with the Furys makes one thing clear: the road back hasn't been smooth at home.

The former heavyweight champion, who is set to fight again on April 11 against Arslanbek Makhmudov, opens the teaser insisting he's 'happily retired' - a claim that quickly unravels as the footage rolls on.

At first, it's all picture-perfect family life. Fury is seen soaking up sunshine on luxury holidays, spending time with wife Paris and their eight children, and even staging a lavish renewal of their wedding vows.

It's the image of a man embracing life beyond the boxing ring, swapping the brutality of the sport for the comforts of Morecambe Bay. But beneath the surface, old habits die hard.

Within moments, Fury is back where he belongs, in the gym. Training sessions intensify, he's shown coaching his sons, and in one clip, he steps into the ring to spar with brother Tommy Fury. It's clear, his retirement was never going to last.

And at home, the decision to fight again doesn't go down quietly.

Tyson Fury might already have one eye on his looming return to the ring but the latest trailer for At Home with the Furys makes one thing clear: the road back hasn't been smooth at home

Paris Fury above in the trailer for At Home with the Furys Two which was released today

In one of the trailer's most striking moments, Paris Fury delivers a brutally honest verdict on her husband's plans to return - bluntly calling him a 't***' as tensions bubble over. It's a glimpse into the very real strain his comeback places on family life, despite the glitz and glamour surrounding them.

Fury had already revealed to Daily Mail Sport that his family did not support his decision to come out of retirement during an interview ahead of last month's press conference announcing his comeback fight.

'My dad stopped speaking to me for a while. My brothers stopped speaking to me, even Paris. Everybody cut me off,' Fury said of the fallout after he chose to end yet another retirement and fight again.

The Gypsy King went on to add: 'Nobody wanted me to return and they made that clear… but, it's my decision and my life.'

Away from boxing, the series also leans heavily into the personal milestones unfolding within the Fury household.

Viewers are given a front-row seat as Fury's daughter Venezuela's partner approaches him to ask for permission to marry her. It's a nerve-wracking moment for any suitor, let alone one involving the Gypsy King. The trailer then cuts to the emotional proposal itself.

According to the official synopsis, the new season follows Fury as he attempts to navigate 'retirement' - even as that notion begins to slip away - by throwing himself into new ventures. From investing in a racehorse to heading off on a family road trip to Monaco, he's searching for purpose beyond the ring.

Meanwhile, Paris is carving out her own path, juggling business ambitions with the chaos of family life, while organising two major events: a lavish sweet sixteen celebration and the couple's headline-grabbing vow renewal.

Paris Fury delivers a brutally honest verdict on her husband's plans to return - bluntly calling him a 't***' as tensions bubble over

Tyson Fury pictured with his wife Paris and their eight children while away on holiday

Noah Price is seen approaching Tyson Fury to ask for permission before later getting down on one knee in front of Venezuela's family and friends

But if the trailer is anything to go by, the real story isn't just about life outside boxing, it's about how impossible Fury finds it to stay away.



Because while fans now know he will step back through the ropes on April 11, At Home with the Furys promises to show exactly how and at what cost that decision was made behind closed doors.

Boxing.title: John Fury makes his feelings on granddaughter Venezuela, 16, and her engagement VERY clear in Fury family's new Netflix series trailer
Boxing.content:
John Fury has shared his reservations about his granddaughter Venezuela Fury's engagement in the new trailer for At Home with the Furys, offering a more traditional view on teenage relationships.

In the clip, the Fury family patriarch reflects on the news that Venezuela, 16, is set to marry her boyfriend Noah Price, stating that in his opinion, teenagers are too young for such commitments.

'On my watch,' he says, 'a 15-year-old should never have a boyfriend, ever.'

The moment features as part of a wider storyline in the upcoming Netflix series, which follows Venezuela's relationship with Price and the reaction from those closest to her.

The teenager revealed she was engaged earlier this year after Price proposed during her sixteenth birthday celebrations, with the scene also teased in the trailer released today.

In it, Price is seen approaching Tyson Fury to ask for permission before later getting down on one knee in front of Venezuela's family and friends. Venezuela looked glamorous for the event as she stepped out in a blue floral-print backless mini dress, Louboutin heels, and a headpiece, as she carried a yellow Chanel bag.

John Fury has shared his reservations about his granddaughter Venezuela Fury's engagement in the new trailer for At Home with the Furys

The teenager revealed she was engaged earlier this year after Price proposed during her sixteenth birthday celebrations, with the scene also teased in the trailer published today

Meanwhile Noah looked dapper in a tailored navy suit with a red tie and handkerchief, as well as brown leather brogues - pulling Venezuela in for a kiss on the cheek after she said yes.

During his conversation with Tyson, the Gypsy King adopts a notably serious tone, pausing to emphasise the weight of his words before asking: 'Marriage is forever. Are you sure this is what you want to do?'.

Venezuela's parents, Tyson and Paris Fury, appear supportive of the relationship despite acknowledging her age. Paris previously said she was 'still in shock' but 'very happy' for the couple, while Tyson is shown giving his approval ahead of the proposal.

However, the same cannot entirely be said of her grandfather, John. Known for his deeply traditional outlook and old-school values, John is understood to be less comfortable with the situation - particularly given Venezuela’s young age.

While early engagements are sometimes part of Traveller and Romany Gypsy life, it’s far from a one-size-fits-all tradition. Customs can vary widely between families, with some welcoming younger relationships, while others prefer to take things slowly.

John, in particular, is very much old school. He has long lived by the values of the Traveller way of life, where family, respect, and clearly defined roles are everything.

He still embraces the traditional nomadic lifestyle, living in his wagon and keeping close to those he loves, and it is this upbringing that shapes his view on relationships.

Given those deeply held beliefs, it’s perhaps no surprise that he is cautious. Even in a culture where young romances are sometimes accepted, John is said to take a firm, old-fashioned stance on commitment - particularly when it involves someone as young as Venezuela.

In it, Price is seen approaching Tyson Fury to ask for permission before later getting down on one knee in front of Venezuela's family and friends

Tyson Fury pictured with his wife Paris and their eight children while away on holiday

The engagement also mirrors Tyson and Paris's own relationship, having first met as teenagers before later marrying and starting a family

The situation has inevitably drawn wider attention due to the couple’s age. In England, the legal minimum age for marriage and civil partnership is 18, meaning the pair would not be able to marry in the near future.

The engagement also mirrors Tyson and Paris's own relationship, having first met as teenagers before later marrying and starting a family.

Venezuela and Price made their relationship public earlier this year, having been seen together at events including race days at Ascot and York.

Noah, who is a boxer and describes himself as an East Midlands belt holder, has since appeared regularly on her social media.



The storyline is expected to form part of the second series of the Netflix show, which continues to follow life inside the Fury household as the family navigate both personal milestones and life in the public eye.

Boxing.title: Jake Paul teases boxing return just three months after having his jaw broken by Anthony Joshua
Boxing.content:
Jake Paul appears set to announce his return to the ring just weeks after undergoing a second surgery on the broken jaw he suffered against Anthony Joshua.

The YouTuber-turned-boxer, 29, had his jaw shattered in two places by Joshua back in December after stepping up to face the former unified heavyweight champion in a dangerous mismatch which sparked fierce controversy.

A lethal right hand floored Paul to the point of no return in the sixth round of the contentious Miami bout, while also leaving him with a sickening injury which has cast doubt over his fighting career.

After having an initial op almost immediately, he was forced to go under the knife for a second time last month as the screws and plates in his jaw were 'coming loose'.

Yet nearly four weeks later, Paul has teased an announcement over his potential boxing return on Wednesday.

The online sensation shared a video of himself hitting the pads and wrote on Monday: 'You won't expect this one hahahaha - announcement coming in 2 days March 18th'.

Jake Paul appears set to announce his boxing return despite undergoing a second jaw surgery

The YouTuber-turned-boxer had his jaw shattered by Anthony Joshua back in December

You won't expect this one hahahaha 😈⚡️announcement coming in 2 days March 18th pic.twitter.com/NT20egDt0p — Jake Paul (@jakepaul) March 16, 2026

At the start of February, Paul's Most Valuable Promotions co-founder Nakisa Bidarian said he is targeting a return to the ring in 2026 - and a move back down to cruiserweight - following his defeat to Joshua.

He drafted in heavyweight AJ at short notice, having initially been preparing for the significantly shorter and lighter Gervonta Davis, who was pulled out of their scheduled bout amid allegations of domestic abuse.

It remains to be seen which opponent Paul chooses next, with a rematch against bitter rival Tommy Fury potentially an option.

Fury, the younger brother of Joshua's heavyweight nemesis Tyson, handed Paul his only other professional defeat when they met in 2023.

Nevertheless, he may not be able to begin sparring again until later this year, meaning a return could come at the end of 2026.

Asked when he expects to fight again, Paul said earlier this month: 'Later this year. I got my second surgery recently.

'So the doctors said like four, five, or six months to even be able to spar to see how the bone is healing.'

Boxing.title: Touching footage shows Anthony Joshua dancing with mums of his two friends who died - after heartfelt speech during Mother's Day party
Boxing.content:
Anthony Joshua danced with the mothers of his two late friends during touching scenes at a Mother's Day party.

At the celebration on Sunday, AJ was filmed dancing with the mums of Sina Ghami and Latif 'Latz' Ayodele.

Sina and 'Latz' were killed last December in a horrendous car accident in Nigeria, while AJ and the driver survived.

Their deaths have had a profound impact on the boxer but he has been seen regularly with their family members and acquitted himself admirably in the aftermath of the tragedy.

Footage of Sunday's Mother's Day celebration shows AJ making a short speech to the assembled guests.

He said: 'To come under one roof and celebrate our mothers, celebrate our brothers, celebrate those that are not with us, and celebrate those that are with us.

Anthony Joshua was seen dancing with the mothers of his two friends who were killed

'Knowing that all of our fates will one day come, we just stay strong until that day. We pray for our transition and just to be great in the afterlife.

'And why I talk about that is because my heart’s for my bros right now, do you know what I mean?

'So, thank you again; why we’ve done this is obviously for my mum and our family, but also for Sina, Latz, Sina’s mum, and Latz’s mum.

'It’s all interconnected and I’m not making too much sense — I’m just speaking from my heart, basically.

'So thank you, mums, for giving me Latz and Sina for so many years and for looking over me, you know? I appreciate it a lot. Thank you very much.'

Joshua shared a number of clips from the event on his Snapchat story and appeared to be in high spirits.

It has been a hugely difficult few months for the former heavyweight champion, who was lucky to escape last year's car wreck with his life, miraculously walking away with minor injuries.

The 36-year-old has paid emotional tributes to his friends on social media and vowed to take care of their families in the aftermath, while he also had their names tattooed on his arm.

Joshua, born in Watford to Nigerian parents, was on holiday in Lagos after his win over Jake Paul in Miami on December 19. In the days after the crash Joshua thanked fans for the support they had shown after the tragedy.

Joshua pictured with Latif Ayodele (left) and Sina Ghami (right)

AJ has dealt with the deaths of his two close friends admirably and been a pillar of strength

In January, AJ spoke for the first time at length about the tragedy with an emotional video on Instagram.

He said: 'The last time I spoke to you guys was in Miami, we had so many plans to wrap up 2025. We went home, to see our families and everything got flipped on its head.

'That was such an unforeseen circumstance that was out of all of our control. And not only did their parents, their uncles, their cousins, their friends and myself, lose two great men. We lost people that we dearly care about and have been major players in all of our lives, major, major players in all of our lives. It's tough. It's really tough.'

'I'm not gonna sit here and show all of my emotions. I know in today's day and age, it's easy to analyse people, pass judgment, but I know what I thought, and I know how I feel.

'I know what my duty is. They were my brothers, my friends, first and foremost. Then we became business partners, we became hustlers, we became generals, we became everything. We became housemates, who were living together.

'You know, I've lost people before, but I don't think I've lost people throughout this journey that I've been on. It's like you think I'm the big guy but I was walking with giants. Protected.'

Boxing.title: Jake Paul suffers embarrassing hygiene gaffe during Donald Trump rally appearance as major wardrobe error exposes long-held insecurity
Boxing.content:
YouTuber-turned-boxer Jake Paul was seen profusely sweating in an appearance with US President Donald Trump at a political rally.

Paul joined Trump on stage at Verst Logistics in Kentucky and was given the chance to address the crowd.

The 29-year-old went viral on social media, not for his comments about Trump and American manufacturing, but instead about his appearance.

Many pointed out vast sweat patches that were visible on Paul's beige suit as he spoke to the crowd.

The patches were particularly prominent under his armpits, which were exposed further when he raised his arms to the crowd.

Paul had previously spoken about how sweating had long been a major insecurity of his.

Jake Paul gets on stage at the Trump rally and has the nastiest pit stains I've ever seen



(it also appears the crowd doesn't know who he is) pic.twitter.com/3Yc071tZzf — Aaron Rupar (@atrupar) March 11, 2026

YouTuber-turned-boxer Jake Paul was seen profusely sweating in an appearance with US President Donald Trump

Paul and his brother Logan had promoted his deodorant brand ahead of his fight with Mike Tyson back in 2024

Paul made the revelation back in 2024 when he launched a deodorant brand W, which he and his brother Logan promoted before and after his bout with Mike Tyson.

'Sweating has been a longtime insecurity of mine,' Paul had said when launching the brand.

'If you know me, you know I'm a clean freak. I always want to smell fresh, but training in Puerto Rico's humid climate makes it a real challenge.

'I needed a product that could keep up with my lifestyle — one that smells amazing but isn't full of junk ingredients, and honestly, one that wasn't from 2006.

'That's why I started W… to create products for guys that will boost their confidence and smell amazing, but also don't cost $20 for a stick of deodorant.'

The W deodorants are claimed to offer 24 hours protection, with the brand website stating: 'With doubled scent load, increased hydration and more sweat-blocking power, our new deodorant and body wash are a monumental breakthrough for mankind (or at least for your morning)'.

During the event, Trump signalled that Paul's influence could eventually shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government in the coming years.

'I'm going to make a prediction that you will be, in the not too distant future, running for political office,' Trump told the fighter and the assembled supporters.

Join the debate Did YOU spot Jake Paul's hygiene gaffe?

Trump had brought Paul onstage during a rally on Wednesday in Kentucky

Trump suggested that Paul could shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government

'And you have my complete and total endorsement.'

Paul had expressed his admiration for Trump credited him with teaching him the fundamental values of grit and resilience.

'We need more factories like this thriving all over the country,' Paul added. And I know [Trump's] going to be the one to bring that here to us.

'I know God is with us. I know he wants us on the right side of history, and everyone here has to do their part.

'And God's got us. Trump's got us. God bless.'

Trump returned the praise, telling the crowd: 'He's a great guy. He's a courageous guy and a talented guy. He's a hell of a fighter, too, by the way.'

The endorsement of Paul's fighting spirit comes as the 29-year-old continues to dominate headlines following his endeavours in the boxing ring over recent years.

Boxing.title: Donald Trump gives Jake Paul shock endorsement for political career as boxer joins President at Kentucky rally
Boxing.content:
President Donald Trump brought boxing star Jake Paul onstage during a rally on Wednesday and made a bold prediction about the social media star's future.

The 29-year-old Ohio native, who has transitioned from a YouTube personality to a major force in the combat sports world, stood alongside the president as the crowd in Hebron, Ky roared in support.

During the event, Trump signaled that Paul's influence could eventually shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government in the coming years.

'I'm going to make a prediction that you will be, in the not too distant future, running for political office,' Trump told the fighter and the assembled supporters.

The president didn't stop at a mere prediction, immediately offering his political backing to the 'Problem Child' should he decide to pursue a seat in the future.

'And you have my complete and total endorsement,' the president added, cementing a public alliance with one of the most polarizing and popular figures in modern media.

President Donald Trump brought boxing star Jake Paul onstage during a rally on Wednesday

Trump suggested that Paul could shift from the boxing ring to the halls of government

Paul expressed his admiration for the president and credited him with teaching him the fundamental values of grit and resilience

Addressing the Kentucky crowd, Paul expressed his admiration for the president and credited him with teaching him the fundamental values of grit and resilience.
